text.title: Pricing Experiment Failure Modes
text.summary: Six failure modes that recur in Stripe-based pricing experiments. Each has a detector and a repair.

failure-mode.description: Grandfathered customer re-billed at the new price — Stripe Plan or Price object was deleted, or the grandfather coupon expired silently, customer churns and posts a critical review.
failure-mode.detector: any subscription with grandfather_eligible = true is billed with the new effective price after renewal OR coupon used_count drops to zero unexpectedly
failure-mode.repair: Immediately credit the difference back to the customer via Stripe (Refund + apology email). Restore the grandfather coupon attachment. Post-incident: switch grandfathering from "coupon stacked on new Price" to "subscription stays on old Price object" — more durable.

failure-mode.description: Statistically never closes — experiment volume too low for the chosen significance threshold, founder extends it for months, status quo becomes the new price by default.
failure-mode.detector: plan.duration_days > 60 AND samples per variant < required-power AND third extension proposed
failure-mode.repair: Force the decision: if the test cannot close at the planned bar, the founder either (a) accepts a wider confidence interval (Bayesian 80% credible interval, or pre-registered effect-size threshold) and decides on that basis, or (b) cancels and reverts. No third extension.

failure-mode.description: Refund storm — price increase announced poorly or grandfather rule missed an audience segment, refund requests spike, Stripe Radar dispute risk rises.
failure-mode.detector: refund_rate during experiment > 2x baseline OR dispute_rate > 0.5%
failure-mode.repair: Terminate experiment immediately, revert to old Price for new signups, process refunds proactively for affected customers, post an apology + correction email. Audit the customer announcement and grandfather rule to identify the missed audience segment.

failure-mode.description: Currency drift — experiment runs on a single USD Price but ~20% of customers see localised currency display from another source (Stripe Checkout auto-localisation, frontend lookup), so the perceived test variant differs from the actual Stripe Price.
failure-mode.detector: plan.currencies_in_test has only USD AND visitor-tracking shows > 5% of trial signups from non-USD regions
failure-mode.repair: Pause the experiment. Create per-currency Price objects matching the variant intent. Re-run with proper currency routing. Existing trial signups during the contaminated window are flagged and excluded from the readout.

failure-mode.description: Stripe Tax misconfiguration — tax_behavior was inclusive in test mode and exclusive in live mode, so first VAT invoice rejects and the customer-support queue explodes.
failure-mode.detector: tax_behavior in plan.variant_new differs from live Stripe Price object OR Stripe Tax shows mismatched jurisdictional default
failure-mode.repair: Pause experiment. Reconfigure tax_behavior on the new Price to match. Issue corrected invoices to any customers billed during the misconfigured window. Document the fix in the decision memo.

failure-mode.description: Decision memo absent — experiment ends, founder is too busy to write the memo, default becomes "leave it running" and the experimental price quietly becomes permanent.
failure-mode.detector: plan.end_date passed by > 14 days AND decision_memo.written_at empty
failure-mode.repair: Calendar reminder T-2 and T+1 to write the memo. If the memo is genuinely impossible (founder unavailable), an automatic revert-to-old-price is triggered as the safer default; the founder writes the memo after returning.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: Customer pressure to "extend the experiment so I can decide later" is not a basis for extension. Either the metric closes the test or it does not; ad-hoc extensions defeat the discipline. The grandfather coupon is the right tool for individual customer hesitation, not the experiment extension.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Post-decision: founder posts a one-paragraph announcement to existing customers explaining the outcome (we adopted / we reverted / we are extending with X change). Transparency is the cheapest trust currency a solo SaaS has; pricing experiments are precisely where it pays out.
